docs.title: Titan CLI
docs.text: Modular development tools orchestrator — Automate Git, GitHub, and Jira workflows through a plugin system with an intuitive terminal UI and optional AI assistance.

docs.title: What is Titan?
docs.text:
Titan is a CLI tool that lets you define and run workflows — sequences of automated steps that combine Git operations, GitHub API calls, Jira queries, shell commands, and AI-generated content into a single, repeatable action.

Instead of running five commands manually every time you open a PR, you run titan and pick a workflow from the menu.

docs.title: Key features
docs.text:
• Workflow engine — Compose atomic steps into automated flows defined in YAML
  • Modern TUI — Interactive terminal interface powered by Textual
  • Plugin system — Built-in Git, GitHub, and Jira plugins; extensible with your own
  • AI integration — Optional AI connections for commit messages, PR descriptions, and issue analysis
  • Project-scoped — Per-project configuration in .titan/config.toml; no global state

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pipx install titan-cli

Then launch from inside any project:

cd /path/to/your-project
titan

On first run, Titan guides you through setting up global preferences and enabling plugins for your project.

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• New to Titan?

    Start here to get Titan installed and run your first workflow.

    → Getting Started

  • Understand how workflows work

    Learn what a workflow is, how steps connect, and how results control execution.

    → Workflow Concepts

  • Explore built-in plugins

    Browse the capabilities of the official Git, GitHub, and Jira plugins.

    → Plugins

  • Build your own workflow

    Hands-on tutorial: extend a built-in workflow with a custom step.

    → Your First Workflow

  • Contribute to Titan

    Set up a development environment and understand the codebase.

    → Contributing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Titan supports AI through configurable connections:

  • Direct Provider connections for:
  • Anthropic
  • OpenAI
  • Gemini
  • LLM Gateway connections for OpenAI-compatible endpoints such as LiteLLM

Configure AI in ~/.titan/config.toml:

[ai]
default_connection = "default"

[ai.connections.default]
name = "My Anthropic"
kind = "direct_provider"
provider = "anthropic"
default_model = "claude-sonnet-4-5"

Titan stores your API key securely in the OS keyring — you'll be prompted for it on first use.

AI is optional. All built-in workflows work without it; AI steps are skipped if no AI connection is configured.

docs.title: Direct Provider
docs.text:
Use this when Titan talks directly to the vendor API.

[ai.connections.personal-openai]
name = "Personal OpenAI"
kind = "direct_provider"
provider = "openai"
default_model = "gpt-5"

docs.title: LLM Gateway
docs.text:
Use this when a single endpoint exposes one or more models through an OpenAI-compatible API.

[ai.connections.work-gateway]
name = "Work Gateway"
kind = "gateway"
gateway_type = "openai_compatible"
base_url = "https://llm.company.com"
default_model = "claude-sonnet-4-5"

docs.title: Workflows
docs.text:
A workflow is a sequence of steps that Titan executes in order. You define workflows in YAML files and run them from the Titan TUI or CLI.

Workflows are the core of Titan. Everything you do — committing, opening a PR, analyzing a Jira ticket — is a workflow.

docs.title: A minimal example
docs.text:
name: "Run tests and commit"
description: "Runs the test suite, then commits if tests pass"

steps:
  - id: run-tests
    name: "Run Tests"
    command: "pytest"

  - id: commit
    name: "Commit"
    plugin: git
    step: create_commit

This workflow runs pytest as a shell command, then calls the create_commit step from the git plugin.

docs.title: YAML structure
docs.text:
name: "My Workflow"           # Required. Shown in the menu.
description: "What it does"  # Optional. Shown below the name.

params:                       # Optional. Default values accessible in all steps.
  draft: false
  assignees: []

hooks:                        # Optional. Named injection points for extending this workflow.
  - before_commit
  - after_push

steps:
  - id: my_step              # Optional. Auto-generated if omitted.
    name: "My Step"          # Optional. Shown in the TUI while running.
    plugin: github           # Which plugin provides this step.
    step: create_pr          # The step function name inside that plugin.
    on_error: continue       # Optional. "fail" (default) or "continue".
    params:                  # Optional. Extra values passed to this step.
      draft: "${draft}"

docs.title: Step types
docs.text: Every step must define exactly one action.

docs.title: Plugin step
docs.text:
Calls a registered step function from a plugin.

- plugin: github
  step: create_pr

Special plugin values:

Value Meaning plugin: git Built-in Git plugin plugin: github Built-in GitHub plugin plugin: jira Built-in Jira plugin plugin: project A step in .titan/steps/ of the current project plugin: user A step in ~/.titan/steps/ (personal steps) plugin: core A built-in Titan step (e.g. ai_code_assistant)

docs.title: Shell command
docs.text:
Runs a shell command directly.

- id: lint
  name: "Run Linter"
  command: "ruff check ."
  on_error: continue    # keep going even if lint fails

Add use_shell: true if your command needs pipes or redirects (cmd1 | cmd2).

docs.title: Nested workflow
docs.text:
Calls another workflow as a step.

- id: commit-first
  workflow: "commit-ai"

Resolution modes:

# Resolved by precedence (project overrides win):
- workflow: "commit-ai"

# Always the base workflow from the git plugin, ignoring any project override:
- workflow: "plugin:git/commit-ai"

Use the plugin: prefix when you explicitly need the base workflow, not a project's customized version.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Workflow-level params define defaults accessible in all steps via ctx.get("key").

Step-level params are merged into the context before that step runs.

Use ${key} to interpolate values from the context at runtime:

params:
  assignees: []

steps:
  - plugin: github
    step: create_issue
    params:
      assignees: "${assignees}"   # resolved from ctx.data at runtime
      labels: "${labels}"

docs.title: Where workflows live
docs.text:
Titan discovers workflows from four sources. When the same workflow name exists in multiple sources, higher priority wins:

Priority Source Location 1 (highest) Project .titan/workflows/*.yaml 2 User ~/.titan/workflows/*.yaml 3 System Bundled with Titan CLI 4 (lowest) Plugin Bundled with each plugin

This means you can override any built-in workflow by creating a file with the same name in .titan/workflows/.

docs.title: Extending workflows
docs.text: Instead of replacing a workflow entirely, you can extend it — injecting extra steps at named hook points.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
A base workflow declares hooks (named injection points):

# Built into the git plugin
name: "Commit with AI"
hooks:
  - before_commit      # ← injection point

steps:
  - plugin: git
    step: get_status

  - hook: before_commit  # ← your steps get injected here

  - plugin: git
    step: ai_generate_commit_message

  - plugin: git
    step: create_commit

Your project extends it by filling those hooks:

# .titan/workflows/commit-ai.yaml
name: "Commit with AI + Linter"
extends: "plugin:git/commit-ai"

hooks:
  before_commit:
    - id: run-lint
      name: "Run Linter"
      command: "ruff check ."
      on_error: continue

Now when you run commit-ai, Titan runs the base workflow with your lint step injected before the commit.

Notes:

  • extends: "plugin:git/commit-ai" targets the base plugin workflow directly
  • extends: "commit-ai" resolves by precedence — could be another project override
  • The after hook is always available, even if not declared in the base, for appending steps at the very end
  • Params are shallow-merged: your params override base params on conflict

docs.title: Step results
docs.text:
Every step function returns one of four result types. The engine checks the result after each step to decide what to do next.

Step returns:
  Success  →  merge metadata into ctx.data  →  continue to next step
  Skip     →  merge metadata into ctx.data  →  continue to next step
  Error    →  check on_error config         →  stop OR continue
  Exit     →  merge metadata into ctx.data  →  STOP entire workflow

docs.title: Success
docs.text:
The step completed. Workflow continues.

return Success("PR created")
return Success("Branch created", metadata={"branch_name": "feat/my-feature"})

metadata is merged into ctx.data so subsequent steps can read it.

docs.title: Skip
docs.text:
The step had nothing to do, but the workflow should continue. Semantically different from Success: nothing happened, the step was not applicable.

return Skip("AI not configured")
return Skip("No commits to push")

docs.title: Error
docs.text:
The step failed. Behavior depends on on_error in the YAML:

on_error Behavior "fail" (default) Workflow stops immediately "continue" Workflow continues to the next step 
return Error("GitHub client not available")
return Error("API rate limit exceeded")

docs.title: Exit
docs.text:
Stops the entire workflow immediately. Not an error — signals "the workflow is no longer needed". The engine converts it to Success for any parent workflow.

return Exit("No changes to commit")   # Nothing to do — stop cleanly
return Exit("No open PRs found")

docs.title: Critical: Skip vs Exit
docs.text:
This is the most common mistake when writing workflow steps.

Exit stops ALL remaining steps, including cleanup steps. Use it only before any resources have been allocated.

Skip continues to the next step. Use it for "nothing to do here, keep going".

docs.title: The wrong way
docs.text:
steps:
  - step: create_worktree    # Creates a worktree on disk
  - step: do_work            # Returns Exit("nothing to do") ← workflow stops here
  - step: cleanup_worktree   # ← NEVER RUNS. Worktree left on disk.

docs.title: The right way
docs.text:
# In do_work step
if not items:
    return Skip("Nothing to do")   # Continues to cleanup_worktree

docs.title: Guaranteed cleanup pattern
docs.text:
To ensure a step always runs (cleanup, teardown):

  1. Use on_error: continue on all steps after resource creation
  2. Use Skip (not Exit) in intermediate steps when they have nothing to do
  3. Use Exit only in early steps where no resources have been created yet
steps:
  # Early steps: Exit is fine here — nothing created yet
  - plugin: github
    step: select_pr_for_review

  # After this, a worktree exists on disk — must guarantee cleanup
  - id: create_worktree
    plugin: github
    step: create_worktree
    on_error: continue          # even if this fails, try to cleanup

  - plugin: github
    step: do_work               # returns Skip (not Exit) when nothing to do
    on_error: continue

  - plugin: github
    step: cleanup_worktree      # ALWAYS runs thanks to Skip + on_error: continue

docs.title: Writing a step function
docs.text:
Step functions are Python functions that follow this signature:

from titan_cli.engine import WorkflowContext, WorkflowResult, Success, Error, Skip, Exit

def my_step(ctx: WorkflowContext) -> WorkflowResult:
    value = ctx.get("some_key")          # read from context
    value = ctx.get("some_key", "default")  # with default

    # ... do work ...

    return Success("Done", metadata={"result": value})

The function name must exactly match the step: field in the YAML.

# .titan/steps/run_linter.py
def run_linter(ctx: WorkflowContext) -> WorkflowResult:  # ← function name = step name
    ...

- plugin: project
  step: run_linter   # ← must match exactly

For a hands-on example, see Your First Workflow.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Coming soon

Detailed architecture documentation is being written. In the meantime, contributors can refer to the internal docs in .claude/docs/ within the repository.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Titan follows a 5-layer plugin architecture:

Steps → Operations → Client → Services → Network
  ↓         ↓          ↓         ↓          ↓
 UI    Business    Public   Data Access   HTTP/CLI
       Logic       API

Each official plugin (Git, GitHub, Jira) follows this structure. See the source in plugins/ for reference implementations.

docs.title: Plugin Documentation Rule
docs.text:
When working on an official plugin, keep its public documentation in sync with the code.

This applies to changes in:

  • New public client functions
  • Removed public client functions
  • Parameter changes in existing public client functions
  • Behavioral changes that affect how an existing function should be used
  • New plugin workflows that expose new capabilities worth documenting

Update the corresponding page in the Plugins section:

  • Git Plugin
  • GitHub Plugin
  • Jira Plugin

At minimum, the documentation should reflect:

  • The operation name
  • How to call it
  • Which parameters are required
  • Which parameters are optional
  • Any important usage constraints

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text: This guide is for contributors who want to work on Titan CLI itself.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • Poetry for dependency management
  • Git
# Install Poetry if you don't have it
pipx install poetry

docs.title: Clone and install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/masorange/titan-cli.git
cd titan-cli

make dev-install

make dev-install does two things:

  1. Runs poetry install — creates a virtualenv at .venv/ with all dependencies, including the three plugins in editable mode
  2. Creates ~/.local/bin/titan-dev — a wrapper script that runs Titan from your local source

docs.title: Run your changes
docs.text:
titan-dev

titan-dev always runs from your local codebase. No reinstall needed after editing Python files.

docs.title: Logging in titan-dev
docs.text:
• titan-dev enables development logging.
  • During TUI execution, logs are written to ~/.local/state/titan/logs/titan.log.
  • If you want live visual debugging with Textual, run titan-dev --devtools and start textual console in another terminal.

Note

titan-dev is only for contributors. End users who install from PyPI get only the titan command.

docs.title: Run tests
docs.text:
make test
# or
poetry run pytest

The test suite covers the main package and all three plugins.

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
titan-cli/
├── titan_cli/              # Core engine, TUI, CLI
│   ├── engine/            # Workflow execution
│   └── ui/tui/            # Textual TUI interface
├── plugins/
│   ├── titan-plugin-git/
│   ├── titan-plugin-github/
│   └── titan-plugin-jira/
├── tests/
└── pyproject.toml

Each plugin is an independent Python package installed in editable mode during development.

docs.title: Docs
docs.text:
To work on this documentation site locally:

make docs-serve

Opens a live-reloading preview at http://localhost:8000. Changes to any file in docs/ are reflected instantly.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or later
  • macOS, Linux, or Windows (WSL2 recommended on Windows)

docs.title: Install with pipx (recommended)
docs.text:
pipx installs Titan in an isolated environment and puts it on your PATH automatically — no virtualenv management needed.

# Install pipx if you don't have it
python3 -m pip install --user pipx
python3 -m pipx ensurepath

# Install Titan
pipx install titan-cli

Verify:

titan --version

docs.title: Install with pip
docs.text:
If you prefer a regular pip install into an existing environment:

pip install titan-cli

Warning

Using pip directly can create dependency conflicts with other packages. pipx is strongly recommended.

docs.title: First launch
docs.text:
Run Titan from inside a project directory:

cd /path/to/your-project
titan

On first launch, two setup wizards run automatically:

  1. Global setup — Configure AI connections. This is optional and can be skipped.
  2. Project setup — Choose a project name and enable the plugins you want (Git, GitHub, Jira).

After setup, the main menu appears and you're ready to run workflows.

docs.title: What gets created
docs.text:
Path Purpose ~/.titan/config.toml Global config: AI connections and credentials .titan/config.toml Project config: enabled plugins and settings

The project config lives at the git repository root, so it works correctly in monorepos.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: → Quick Start — Learn the basics of the Titan interface.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide assumes Titan is already installed. If not, see Installation.

docs.title: Launch Titan
docs.text:
Run titan from inside any configured project:

cd /path/to/your-project
titan

Titan resolves the project root from the git repository root, so you can run it from any subdirectory of a monorepo.

docs.title: The main menu
docs.text:
After launch you'll see the main menu with a list of available workflows. These come from:

  • Built-in plugin workflows (Git, GitHub, Jira)
  • Your project's own workflows in .titan/workflows/

Use the arrow keys or type to filter, then press Enter to run a workflow.

docs.title: Run a workflow
docs.text:
Workflows are interactive — they guide you through each step with prompts and confirmations.

For example, the Commit with AI workflow (from the Git plugin):

  1. Shows you the current git diff
  2. Lets you stage changes interactively
  3. Generates a commit message with AI (if configured)
  4. Lets you edit the message before committing

At any point you can press Escape or Ctrl+C to cancel.

docs.title: Enable or disable plugins
docs.text:
Edit .titan/config.toml in your project root:

[project]
name = "my-project"

[plugins.git]
enabled = true

[plugins.github]
enabled = true

[plugins.jira]
enabled = false

docs.title: Configure AI
docs.text:
Edit ~/.titan/config.toml:

[ai]
default_connection = "default"

[ai.connections.default]
name = "My Anthropic"
kind = "direct_provider"
provider = "anthropic"
default_model = "claude-sonnet-4-5"

Titan will prompt for your API key on first use and store it securely in your OS keyring.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: → Your First Workflow — Build a custom workflow from scratch.

docs.title: Your First Workflow
docs.text:
In this tutorial you'll build a custom workflow that runs a linter before every commit. You'll learn how to:

  • Write a custom step in Python
  • Extend a built-in workflow using hooks
  • Run your workflow from Titan

Prerequisites: Titan installed and configured in a project with the git plugin enabled. If not, see Installation.

docs.title: The scenario
docs.text:
The built-in commit-ai workflow commits your changes with an AI-generated message. You want to run ruff (a Python linter) before the commit happens, and skip it gracefully if ruff isn't installed.

You'll inject your linter step into the before_commit hook that commit-ai already exposes.

docs.title: Step 1 — Write the step function
docs.text:
Create the file .titan/steps/run_linter.py in your project:

import subprocess
from titan_cli.engine import WorkflowContext, WorkflowResult, Success, Skip, Error


def run_linter(ctx: WorkflowContext) -> WorkflowResult:
    """Run ruff linter. Skip gracefully if ruff is not installed."""

    # Check ruff is available
    check = subprocess.run(
        ["ruff", "--version"],
        capture_output=True,
    )
    if check.returncode != 0:
        return Skip("ruff not found — skipping lint")

    # Run the linter
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["ruff", "check", "."],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )

    if result.returncode != 0:
        ctx.textual.text(result.stdout)
        return Error("Linting failed — fix errors before committing")

    return Success("Linting passed")

A few things to notice:

  • The function name (run_linter) must match the step: field in the YAML exactly.
  • We return Skip when ruff isn't available — this lets the workflow continue without failing.
  • We return Error when lint fails — by default this stops the workflow (no commit happens).
  • We return Success when everything is fine.

docs.title: Step 2 — Create the workflow file
docs.text:
Create .titan/workflows/commit-ai.yaml:

name: "Commit with AI + Linter"
description: "Runs ruff before committing with an AI-generated message"
extends: "plugin:git/commit-ai"

hooks:
  before_commit:
    - id: run-lint
      name: "Run Linter"
      plugin: project
      step: run_linter
      on_error: continue   # remove this line if you want lint failures to block the commit

What this does:

  • extends: "plugin:git/commit-ai" — takes the base workflow from the git plugin
  • hooks.before_commit — injects your step at the before_commit injection point
  • plugin: project — tells Titan to look for run_linter in .titan/steps/
  • on_error: continue — even if linting fails, the commit step still runs (remove this to make lint failures block commits)

Because this file is named commit-ai.yaml and lives in .titan/workflows/, it overrides the built-in commit-ai workflow for this project. Titan's precedence rules mean project workflows always win.

docs.title: Step 3 — Run it
docs.text:
titan

Select Commit with AI + Linter from the menu. The workflow runs your lint step before generating the commit message.

You'll see each step in the TUI as it executes. If linting fails (and you kept on_error: continue), you'll see the lint errors but the commit step will still run. Remove that line to make lint failures stop the workflow.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
You've seen the two core building blocks: a step (Python function) and a workflow (YAML that sequences steps). From here you can:

  • Add more steps to .titan/steps/ and wire them into workflows
  • Create a completely new workflow (no extends) for project-specific automation
  • Use ctx.get() and metadata to pass data between steps
  • Add AI calls using ctx.ai for generated content

Read Workflow Concepts for the full reference on step types, result types, parameters, and the Skip vs Exit distinction.

docs.title: Git Plugin
docs.text:
The Git plugin adds core Git operations to Titan through a high-level client and reusable workflows. It covers branches, commits, status inspection, diffs, remotes, stashes, tags, and worktrees.

This page documents the plugin from a functional point of view, while also showing how each capability is called and which parameters it needs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
To use the Git plugin in a project:

  • Enable the git plugin in .titan/config.toml
  • Install the git CLI and make sure it is available in PATH
  • Run Titan inside a Git repository

Example project configuration:

[plugins.git]
enabled = true

[plugins.git.config]
main_branch = "main"
default_remote = "origin"

docs.title: Accessing the client
docs.text:
In Titan code, the public entry point is the Git plugin client:

git_plugin = config.registry.get_plugin("git")
client = git_plugin.get_client()

The client returns ClientResult[...] values. In practice, this means each call can succeed with data or return an error result.

docs.title: Get the current branch
docs.text:
Returns the currently checked out branch name.

Call:

client.get_current_branch()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: List branches
docs.text:
Returns local branches, or remote branches when requested.

Call:

client.get_branches(remote=False)

Parameters:

  • remote: Optional. Set to True to list remote branches instead of local ones.

docs.title: Create a branch
docs.text:
Creates a new branch from a starting point.

Call:

client.create_branch(branch_name="feature/search", start_point="HEAD")

Parameters:

  • branch_name: Required. New branch name.
  • start_point: Optional. Git ref to branch from.

docs.title: Delete a branch
docs.text:
Deletes a branch directly.

Call:

client.delete_branch(branch="feature/search", force=False)

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch name to delete.
  • force: Optional. Force deletion.

docs.title: Delete a branch safely
docs.text:
Deletes a branch only if it is not considered protected.

Call:

client.safe_delete_branch(branch="feature/search", force=False)

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch name to delete.
  • force: Optional. Force deletion.

docs.title: Checkout a branch
docs.text:
Switches the repository to another branch.

Call:

client.checkout("feature/search")

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch name to checkout.

docs.title: Check whether a branch exists on remote
docs.text:
Checks if a branch already exists in a remote repository.

Call:

client.branch_exists_on_remote(branch="feature/search", remote="origin")

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch name.
  • remote: Optional. Remote name.

docs.title: Update a branch from remote
docs.text:
Fetches and fast-forwards a branch from the remote.

Call:

client.update_branch(branch="main", remote="origin")

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch to update.
  • remote: Optional. Remote name. Defaults to the configured default remote.

docs.title: Update from the configured main branch
docs.text:
Updates the current branch from the configured main branch.

Call:

client.update_from_main()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Checkout safely
docs.text:
Stores the current branch, optionally stashes local changes, and then switches branches.

Call:

client.safe_checkout(branch="feature/search", auto_stash=True)

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch to checkout.
  • auto_stash: Optional. Automatically stash uncommitted changes first.

docs.title: Return to the original branch
docs.text:
Returns to the branch previously saved by safe_checkout.

Call:

client.return_to_original_branch()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Check whether a branch is protected
docs.text:
Checks if a branch name is treated as protected by the client.

Call:

client.is_protected_branch("main")

Parameters:

  • branch: Required. Branch name.

docs.title: Create a commit
docs.text:
Creates a commit from the current working tree.

Call:

client.commit(message="feat: add search filters", all=False, no_verify=True)

Parameters:

  • message: Required. Commit message.
  • all: Optional. Include tracked modified files automatically.
  • no_verify: Optional. Skip Git hooks.

docs.title: Create a commit for specific files
docs.text:
Creates a commit only for the files passed in the call.

Call:

client.commit_files(
    files=["src/search.py", "tests/test_search.py"],
    message="test: cover search filters",
    no_verify=True,
)

Parameters:

  • files: Required. Files to include in the commit.
  • message: Required. Commit message.
  • no_verify: Optional. Skip Git hooks.

docs.title: Get the current commit SHA
docs.text:
Returns the SHA of HEAD.

Call:

client.get_current_commit()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the commit SHA for a ref
docs.text:
Resolves any Git ref to its commit SHA.

Call:

client.get_commit_sha("HEAD~1")

Parameters:

  • ref: Required. Any Git ref.

docs.title: Get commits versus base
docs.text:
Returns commit messages from the base branch up to HEAD.

Call:

client.get_commits_vs_base()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get commits in a branch compared to another branch
docs.text:
Returns the commits present in the head branch but not in the base branch.

Call:

client.get_branch_commits(base_branch="main", head_branch="feature/search")

Parameters:

  • base_branch: Required. Base branch.
  • head_branch: Required. Head branch.

docs.title: Get commits between refs
docs.text:
Returns commits reachable from one ref but not another.

Call:

client.get_commits_between_refs(base_ref="origin/main", head_ref="HEAD")

Parameters:

  • base_ref: Required. Base ref.
  • head_ref: Optional. Head ref.

docs.title: Count commits ahead of a base branch
docs.text:
Counts how many commits the current branch is ahead of another branch.

Call:

client.count_commits_ahead(base_branch="main")

Parameters:

  • base_branch: Optional. Base branch to compare against.

docs.title: Count unpushed commits
docs.text:
Counts how many commits have not been pushed to the remote yet.

Call:

client.count_unpushed_commits(branch=None, remote="origin")

Parameters:

  • branch: Optional. Branch to inspect. Uses the current branch when omitted.
  • remote: Optional. Remote name.

docs.title: Get repository status
docs.text:
Returns a structured view of the current repository state.

Call:

client.get_status()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Check for uncommitted changes
docs.text:
Returns whether the repository has local changes not yet committed.

Call:

client.has_uncommitted_changes()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get a diff between refs
docs.text:
Returns the diff between two refs.

Call:

client.get_diff(base_ref="origin/main", head_ref="HEAD")

Parameters:

  • base_ref: Required. Base ref.
  • head_ref: Optional. Head ref.

docs.title: Get the full uncommitted diff
docs.text:
Returns the diff of all local changes.

Call:

client.get_uncommitted_diff()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the staged diff
docs.text:
Returns only the staged changes.

Call:

client.get_staged_diff()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the unstaged diff
docs.text:
Returns only the unstaged changes.

Call:

client.get_unstaged_diff()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the diff for one file
docs.text:
Returns the diff for a single file path.

Call:

client.get_file_diff("src/search.py")

Parameters:

  • file_path: Required. File path to inspect.

docs.title: Get the diff between branches
docs.text:
Returns the diff between two branches, with configurable context lines.

Call:

client.get_branch_diff(
    base_branch="main",
    head_branch="feature/search",
    context_lines=3,
    use_remote=False,
)

Parameters:

  • base_branch: Required. Base branch.
  • head_branch: Required. Head branch.
  • context_lines: Optional. Number of diff context lines.
  • use_remote: Optional. Treat both branches as remote refs.

docs.title: Get a diff stat between refs
docs.text:
Returns a summary of file-level changes between refs.

Call:

client.get_diff_stat(base_ref="origin/main", head_ref="HEAD")

Parameters:

  • base_ref: Required. Base ref.
  • head_ref: Optional. Head ref.

docs.title: Get the diff stat for uncommitted changes
docs.text:
Returns a compact summary of local changes.

Call:

client.get_uncommitted_diff_stat()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the diff stat between branches
docs.text:
Returns a compact summary of changes between two branches.

Call:

client.get_branch_diff_stat(base_branch="main", head_branch="feature/search")

Parameters:

  • base_branch: Required. Base branch.
  • head_branch: Required. Head branch.

docs.title: Push to a remote
docs.text:
Pushes commits to a remote branch.

Call:

client.push(remote="origin", branch="feature/search", set_upstream=True, tags=False)

Parameters:

  • remote: Optional. Remote name.
  • branch: Optional. Branch to push. Uses the current branch when omitted.
  • set_upstream: Optional. Set the upstream tracking branch.
  • tags: Optional. Push tags too.

docs.title: Push a tag
docs.text:
Pushes a single tag to a remote.

Call:

client.push_tag(tag_name="v1.2.0", remote="origin")

Parameters:

  • tag_name: Required. Tag name.
  • remote: Optional. Remote name.

docs.title: Pull from a remote
docs.text:
Pulls changes from a remote branch.

Call:

client.pull(remote="origin", branch="main")

Parameters:

  • remote: Optional. Remote name.
  • branch: Optional. Branch to pull.

docs.title: Fetch from a remote
docs.text:
Fetches changes without merging them.

Call:

client.fetch(remote="origin", branch="main", all=False)

Parameters:

  • remote: Optional. Remote name.
  • branch: Optional. Branch to fetch.
  • all: Optional. Fetch all remotes.

docs.title: Extract GitHub repository info
docs.text:
Parses the remote URL and returns the GitHub owner and repository name when available.

Call:

client.get_github_repo_info()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Create a stash
docs.text:
Stashes current local changes.

Call:

client.stash_push(message="temporary local changes")

Parameters:

  • message: Optional. Stash message.

docs.title: Pop a stash
docs.text:
Applies and removes a stash.

Call:

client.stash_pop(stash_ref=None)

Parameters:

  • stash_ref: Optional. Specific stash reference.

docs.title: Find a stash by message
docs.text:
Looks up a stash using its message.

Call:

client.find_stash_by_message("temporary local changes")

Parameters:

  • message: Required. Stash message to search for.

docs.title: Restore a stash by message
docs.text:
Finds a stash by message and restores it.

Call:

client.restore_stash("temporary local changes")

Parameters:

  • message: Required. Stash message to restore.

docs.title: Create a tag
docs.text:
Creates an annotated tag.

Call:

client.create_tag(tag_name="v1.2.0", message="Release 1.2.0", ref="HEAD")

Parameters:

  • tag_name: Required. Tag name.
  • message: Required. Tag annotation message.
  • ref: Optional. Ref to tag.

docs.title: Delete a tag
docs.text:
Deletes a local tag.

Call:

client.delete_tag("v1.2.0")

Parameters:

  • tag_name: Required. Tag name.

docs.title: Check whether a tag exists locally
docs.text:
Checks if a tag is present in the local repository.

Call:

client.tag_exists("v1.2.0")

Parameters:

  • tag_name: Required. Tag name.

docs.title: Check whether a tag exists on remote
docs.text:
Checks if a tag exists in a remote repository.

Call:

client.remote_tag_exists(tag_name="v1.2.0", remote="origin")

Parameters:

  • tag_name: Required. Tag name.
  • remote: Optional. Remote name.

docs.title: List tags
docs.text:
Returns the tags available in the repository.

Call:

client.list_tags()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Create a worktree
docs.text:
Creates a new Git worktree.

Call:

client.create_worktree(
    path="../repo-search-worktree",
    branch="feature/search",
    create_branch=False,
    detached=False,
)

Parameters:

  • path: Required. Filesystem path for the worktree.
  • branch: Required. Branch to attach to the worktree.
  • create_branch: Optional. Create the branch if needed.
  • detached: Optional. Create the worktree in detached mode.

docs.title: Remove a worktree
docs.text:
Removes an existing worktree.

Call:

client.remove_worktree(path="../repo-search-worktree", force=False)

Parameters:

  • path: Required. Worktree path.
  • force: Optional. Force removal.

docs.title: List worktrees
docs.text:
Returns all worktrees for the repository.

Call:

client.list_worktrees()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Run a Git command in a worktree
docs.text:
Runs a Git command inside a specific worktree.

Call:

client.run_in_worktree(worktree_path="../repo-search-worktree", args=["status", "--short"])

Parameters:

  • worktree_path: Required. Worktree path.
  • args: Required. Git arguments to execute.

docs.title: Get recent commits from a worktree
docs.text:
Returns recent commits from a specific worktree.

Call:

client.get_commits(worktree_path="../repo-search-worktree", limit=10)

Parameters:

  • worktree_path: Required. Worktree path.
  • limit: Optional. Maximum number of commits.

docs.title: Get the worktree diff stat
docs.text:
Returns a diff summary for local changes in a worktree.

Call:

client.get_worktree_diff_stat(worktree_path="../repo-search-worktree")

Parameters:

  • worktree_path: Required. Worktree path.

docs.title: Checkout a branch in a worktree
docs.text:
Checks out, or creates, a branch inside a specific worktree.

Call:

client.checkout_branch_in_worktree(
    worktree_path="../repo-search-worktree",
    branch_name="feature/search",
    force=False,
)

Parameters:

  • worktree_path: Required. Worktree path.
  • branch_name: Required. Branch name.
  • force: Optional. Force the checkout.

docs.title: Commit changes in a worktree
docs.text:
Stages and commits changes inside a worktree.

Call:

client.commit_in_worktree(
    worktree_path="../repo-search-worktree",
    message="feat: add search filters",
    add_all=True,
    no_verify=False,
)

Parameters:

  • worktree_path: Required. Worktree path.
  • message: Required. Commit message.
  • add_all: Optional. Stage all tracked changes before committing.
  • no_verify: Optional. Skip Git hooks.

docs.title: Push from a worktree
docs.text:
Pushes a branch from a specific worktree to a remote.

Call:

client.push_from_worktree(
    worktree_path="../repo-search-worktree",
    branch="feature/search",
    remote="origin",
)

Parameters:

  • worktree_path: Required. Worktree path.
  • branch: Required. Branch to push.
  • remote: Optional. Remote name.

docs.title: Related workflows
docs.text:
The Git plugin ships with workflows that use these capabilities directly:

  • commit-ai: Checks repository status, shows a summary of local changes, generates a commit message with AI, creates the commit, and pushes it

These workflows can be used as-is or extended from .titan/workflows/.

docs.title: GitHub Plugin
docs.text:
The GitHub plugin adds GitHub operations to Titan through a high-level client and reusable workflows. It covers pull requests, reviews, issues, releases, teams, and repository metadata.

This page documents the plugin from a functional point of view, while also showing how each capability is called and which parameters it needs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
To use the GitHub plugin in a project:

  • Enable the github plugin in .titan/config.toml
  • Enable the git plugin, because the GitHub plugin depends on it
  • Use a GitHub repository that is either configured explicitly or detectable from the git remote
  • Install and authenticate the gh CLI

Example project configuration:

[plugins.git]
enabled = true

[plugins.github]
enabled = true

[plugins.github.config]
repo_owner = "example-org"
repo_name = "example-repo"
default_branch = "main"
pr_template_path = ".github/pull_request_template.md"
auto_assign_prs = true

docs.title: Accessing the client
docs.text:
In Titan code, the public entry point is the GitHub plugin client:

github_plugin = config.registry.get_plugin("github")
client = github_plugin.get_client()

The client returns ClientResult[...] values. In practice, this means each call can succeed with data or return an error result.

docs.title: Create a pull request
docs.text:
Creates a new pull request from a head branch into a base branch.

Call:

client.create_pull_request(
    title="Add search filters",
    body="## Summary\n- adds filter controls\n- updates results state",
    base="main",
    head="feature/search-filters",
    draft=False,
    assignees=["alice"],
    reviewers=["bob", "example-org/backend-team"],
    labels=["feature", "ui"],
    excluded_reviewers=["carol"],
)

Parameters:

  • title: Required. Pull request title.
  • body: Required. Pull request description.
  • base: Required. Target branch.
  • head: Required. Source branch.
  • draft: Optional. Whether to create the PR as draft.
  • assignees: Optional. GitHub usernames to assign.
  • reviewers: Optional. Usernames or teams.
  • labels: Optional. Labels to apply.
  • excluded_reviewers: Optional. Usernames to exclude after team expansion.

docs.title: Get a pull request
docs.text:
Fetches a single pull request by number.

Call:

client.get_pull_request(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: List pull requests pending review
docs.text:
Returns PRs that still need your review.

Call:

client.list_pending_review_prs(max_results=25, include_team_reviews=True)

Parameters:

  • max_results: Optional. Maximum number of PRs to return.
  • include_team_reviews: Optional. Include PRs requested from your teams.

docs.title: List your pull requests
docs.text:
Returns pull requests created by the authenticated user.

Call:

client.list_my_prs(state="open", max_results=25)

Parameters:

  • state: Optional. PR state such as open, closed, or merged.
  • max_results: Optional. Maximum number of PRs to return.

docs.title: List all pull requests
docs.text:
Returns pull requests from the repository without filtering by author.

Call:

client.list_all_prs(state="open", max_results=50)

Parameters:

  • state: Optional. PR state such as open, closed, or merged.
  • max_results: Optional. Maximum number of PRs to return.

docs.title: Read a pull request diff
docs.text:
Returns the pull request diff as text.

Call:

client.get_pr_diff(pr_number=123, context_lines=3)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • context_lines: Optional. Number of diff context lines.

docs.title: Read patches for specific files
docs.text:
Returns patch text only for selected files in a PR.

Call:

client.get_pr_file_patches(123, ["src/search.py", "tests/test_search.py"])

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • file_paths: Required. List of paths to extract patches for.

docs.title: List changed files
docs.text:
Returns the file paths changed in a pull request.

Call:

client.get_pr_files(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: List changed files with stats
docs.text:
Returns each changed file together with additions, deletions, and status.

Call:

client.get_pr_files_with_stats(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: Checkout a pull request locally
docs.text:
Checks out the pull request branch in the local repository.

Call:

client.checkout_pr(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: Add a PR comment
docs.text:
Adds a general comment to a pull request.

Call:

client.add_comment(123, "Please add test coverage for the empty state.")

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • body: Required. Comment body.

docs.title: Get the latest PR commit SHA
docs.text:
Returns the latest commit SHA associated with the pull request.

Call:

client.get_pr_commit_sha(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: Merge a pull request
docs.text:
Merges a pull request using the selected merge strategy.

Call:

client.merge_pr(
    pr_number=123,
    merge_method="squash",
    commit_title="Add search filters",
    commit_message="Adds filtering controls and updates result handling.",
)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • merge_method: Optional. Merge strategy such as merge, squash, or rebase.
  • commit_title: Optional. Merge commit title.
  • commit_message: Optional. Merge commit message.

docs.title: Get review threads
docs.text:
Returns code review threads for a pull request.

Call:

client.get_pr_review_threads(pr_number=123, include_resolved=True)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • include_resolved: Optional. Include resolved threads.

docs.title: Resolve a review thread
docs.text:
Marks a review thread as resolved.

Call:

client.resolve_review_thread("THREAD_NODE_ID")

Parameters:

  • thread_node_id: Required. GraphQL node ID of the thread.

docs.title: Get reviews for a pull request
docs.text:
Returns submitted reviews such as approvals, change requests, and comments.

Call:

client.get_pr_reviews(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: Create a draft review
docs.text:
Creates a draft review before submitting it.

Call:

client.create_draft_review(
    pr_number=123,
    payload={
        "body": "I left a few comments.",
        "comments": [],
    },
)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • payload: Required. Review payload to send to GitHub.

docs.title: Submit a review
docs.text:
Submits a review event, optionally using an existing draft review.

Call:

client.submit_review(
    pr_number=123,
    review_id=456,
    event="APPROVE",
    body="Looks good to me.",
)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • review_id: Optional. Draft review ID, or None to submit directly.
  • event: Required. Review event such as APPROVE, REQUEST_CHANGES, or COMMENT.
  • body: Optional. Review summary text.

docs.title: Delete a draft review
docs.text:
Deletes an existing draft review.

Call:

client.delete_review(pr_number=123, review_id=456)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • review_id: Required. Draft review ID.

docs.title: Reply to a review comment
docs.text:
Adds a reply to an existing PR review comment.

Call:

client.reply_to_comment(
    pr_number=123,
    comment_id=789,
    body="Updated in the latest commit.",
)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • comment_id: Required. Comment ID to reply to.
  • body: Required. Reply text.

docs.title: Get general PR comments
docs.text:
Returns PR comments that are not attached to a code line.

Call:

client.get_pr_general_comments(123)

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.

docs.title: Add a general issue-style comment to a PR
docs.text:
Adds a non-inline comment to the pull request conversation.

Call:

client.add_issue_comment(123, "This is ready for another pass.")

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • body: Required. Comment body.

docs.title: Request or re-request review
docs.text:
Requests review from one or more users.

Call:

client.request_pr_review(123, reviewers=["alice", "bob"])

Parameters:

  • pr_number: Required. Pull request number.
  • reviewers: Optional. List of GitHub usernames.

docs.title: Create an issue
docs.text:
Creates a new GitHub issue.

Call:

client.create_issue(
    title="Search results are not paginated",
    body="The results page should support server-side pagination.",
    assignees=["alice"],
    labels=["bug", "backend"],
)

Parameters:

  • title: Required. Issue title.
  • body: Required. Issue body.
  • assignees: Optional. Assignee usernames.
  • labels: Optional. Labels to apply.

docs.title: List repository labels
docs.text:
Returns the labels available in the repository.

Call:

client.list_labels()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Create a release
docs.text:
Creates a GitHub release from a tag.

Call:

client.create_release(
    tag_name="v1.2.0",
    title="v1.2.0",
    notes="Release notes for version 1.2.0.",
    generate_notes=False,
    verify_tag=True,
    prerelease=False,
)

Parameters:

  • tag_name: Required. Git tag to release.
  • title: Optional. Release title.
  • notes: Optional. Release notes.
  • generate_notes: Optional. Let GitHub generate the notes automatically.
  • verify_tag: Optional. Verify that the tag exists before creating the release.
  • prerelease: Optional. Mark the release as prerelease.

docs.title: List team members
docs.text:
Returns the members of a GitHub team.

Call:

client.list_team_members("example-org/backend-team")

Parameters:

  • team_slug: Required. Team identifier in org/team format.

This is also used internally when a pull request is created with team reviewers.

docs.title: Read the PR template
docs.text:
Returns the configured PR template content when one is available.

Call:

client.get_pr_template()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the current GitHub user
docs.text:
Returns the authenticated GitHub username.

Call:

client.get_current_user()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get a user's display name
docs.text:
Returns the display name for a GitHub login, falling back to the login if needed.

Call:

client.get_user_display_name("alice")

Parameters:

  • login: Required. GitHub username.

docs.title: Get the current user's display name
docs.text:
Returns the display name of the authenticated user.

Call:

client.get_current_user_display_name()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get the default branch
docs.text:
Returns the repository default branch from Titan config, GitHub metadata, or local git configuration.

Call:

client.get_default_branch()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Related workflows
docs.text:
The GitHub plugin ships with workflows that use these capabilities directly:

  • create-pr-ai: Creates a pull request after committing and pushing changes, with AI-generated PR content.
  • create-issue-ai: Creates a GitHub issue from an AI-suggested title and description.
  • respond-pr-comments: Helps review pending comments, reply to them, and request another review.

These workflows can be used as-is or extended from .titan/workflows/.

docs.title: Jira Plugin
docs.text:
The Jira plugin adds Jira operations to Titan through a high-level client and reusable workflows. It covers issue lookup, JQL search, comments, transitions, issue creation, metadata discovery, links, and AI-assisted issue workflows.

This page documents the plugin from a functional point of view, while also showing how each capability is called and which parameters it needs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
To use the Jira plugin in a project:

  • Enable the jira plugin in .titan/config.toml
  • Configure Jira connection settings such as base_url and email
  • Store the Jira API token in Titan secrets
  • Optionally configure a default_project so project-specific operations can work without passing a project key every time

Example project configuration:

[plugins.jira]
enabled = true

[plugins.jira.config]
base_url = "https://your-company.atlassian.net"
email = "developer@example.com"
default_project = "APP"

The API token is stored in secrets, not in the config file.

docs.title: Accessing the client
docs.text:
In Titan code, the public entry point is the Jira plugin client:

jira_plugin = config.registry.get_plugin("jira")
client = jira_plugin.get_client()

The client returns ClientResult[...] values. In practice, this means each call can succeed with data or return an error result.

docs.title: Get an issue
docs.text:
Returns a single Jira issue by key.

Call:

client.get_issue(key="APP-123", expand=["changelog"])

Parameters:

  • key: Required. Issue key.
  • expand: Optional. Extra Jira expansions to request.

docs.title: Search issues with JQL
docs.text:
Searches for issues using a JQL query.

Call:

client.search_issues(
    jql='project = APP AND status = "To Do"',
    max_results=20,
    fields=["summary", "status", "assignee"],
)

Parameters:

  • jql: Required. JQL query string.
  • max_results: Optional. Maximum number of issues to return.
  • fields: Optional. Fields to request from Jira.

docs.title: Create an issue
docs.text:
Creates a new Jira issue.

Call:

client.create_issue(
    issue_type="Task",
    summary="Add server-side pagination",
    description="The results endpoint should support pagination.",
    project="APP",
    assignee="developer@example.com",
    labels=["backend", "api"],
    priority="High",
    fields={"components": [{"name": "Search"}]},
)

Parameters:

  • issue_type: Required. Issue type name such as Bug, Story, or Task.
  • summary: Required. Issue summary.
  • description: Optional. Issue description.
  • project: Optional. Project key. Uses the configured default project when omitted.
  • assignee: Optional. Assignee username or email.
  • labels: Optional. Labels to apply.
  • priority: Optional. Priority name.
  • fields: Optional. Additional Jira fields to merge into the payload.

docs.title: Create a subtask
docs.text:
Creates a subtask under an existing parent issue.

Call:

client.create_subtask(
    parent_key="APP-123",
    summary="Add API tests",
    description="Cover pagination and empty-state cases.",
)

Parameters:

  • parent_key: Required. Parent issue key.
  • summary: Required. Subtask summary.
  • description: Optional. Subtask description.

This operation requires a configured default project.

docs.title: Get a project
docs.text:
Returns a Jira project by key.

Call:

client.get_project(key="APP")

Parameters:

  • key: Optional. Project key. Uses the configured default project when omitted.

docs.title: List projects
docs.text:
Returns all Jira projects accessible to the current user.

Call:

client.list_projects()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Get issue comments
docs.text:
Returns the comments for a Jira issue.

Call:

client.get_comments("APP-123")

Parameters:

  • issue_key: Required. Issue key.

docs.title: Add a comment
docs.text:
Adds a comment to a Jira issue.

Call:

client.add_comment("APP-123", "The API contract has been updated.")

Parameters:

  • issue_key: Required. Issue key.
  • body: Required. Comment text.

docs.title: Get available transitions
docs.text:
Returns the transitions available for a Jira issue.

Call:

client.get_transitions("APP-123")

Parameters:

  • issue_key: Required. Issue key.

docs.title: Transition an issue
docs.text:
Moves an issue to another Jira status.

Call:

client.transition_issue(
    issue_key="APP-123",
    new_status="In Progress",
    comment="Starting implementation.",
)

Parameters:

  • issue_key: Required. Issue key.
  • new_status: Required. Target status name.
  • comment: Optional. Comment to add while transitioning.

docs.title: Get issue types
docs.text:
Returns the issue types available for a project.

Call:

client.get_issue_types(project_key="APP")

Parameters:

  • project_key: Optional. Project key. Uses the configured default project when omitted.

docs.title: List project statuses
docs.text:
Returns the statuses available for a project.

Call:

client.list_statuses(project_key="APP")

Parameters:

  • project_key: Optional. Project key. Uses the configured default project when omitted.

docs.title: Get the current Jira user
docs.text:
Returns information about the authenticated Jira user.

Call:

client.get_current_user()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: List project versions
docs.text:
Returns the versions configured for a project.

Call:

client.list_project_versions(project_key="APP")

Parameters:

  • project_key: Optional. Project key. Uses the configured default project when omitted.

docs.title: Get priorities
docs.text:
Returns the priorities available in Jira.

Call:

client.get_priorities()

Parameters:

  • No parameters.

docs.title: Link two issues
docs.text:
Creates a Jira link between two issues.

Call:

client.link_issue(
    inward_issue="APP-123",
    outward_issue="APP-456",
    link_type="Relates",
)

Parameters:

  • inward_issue: Required. Source issue key.
  • outward_issue: Required. Target issue key.
  • link_type: Optional. Jira link type.

docs.title: Add a remote link
docs.text:
Adds an external link, such as a pull request or document, to a Jira issue.

Call:

client.add_remote_link(
    issue_key="APP-123",
    url="https://example.com/pull/123",
    title="Pull Request 123",
    relationship="relates to",
)

Parameters:

  • issue_key: Required. Issue key.
  • url: Required. External URL to link.
  • title: Required. Link title.
  • relationship: Optional. Relationship description.

docs.title: Related workflows
docs.text:
The Jira plugin ships with workflows that use these capabilities directly:

  • analyze-jira-issues: Searches open issues, lets the user select one, and analyzes it with AI
  • create-generic-issue: Guides the user through issue creation, enhances the content with AI, and creates the Jira issue

These workflows can be used as-is or extended from .titan/workflows/.

docs.title: Built-in plugins
docs.text:
Titan ships with three official plugins:

Plugin Description git Smart commits, branch management, AI-powered commit messages github Create PRs with AI descriptions, manage issues, code reviews jira Search issues, AI-powered analysis, workflow automation

Enable them per-project in .titan/config.toml:

[plugins.git]
enabled = true

[plugins.github]
enabled = true

docs.title: Community plugins
docs.text:
Titan also supports community plugins from external repositories.

There are currently two source channels:

  • stable: pin a plugin version in the project config using a git tag or commit
  • dev_local: use a local checkout of a plugin repository during development

The shared stable pin lives in .titan/config.toml:

[plugins.custom]
enabled = true

[plugins.custom.source]
channel = "stable"
repo_url = "https://github.com/user/titan-plugin-custom"
requested_ref = "v1.2.0"
resolved_commit = "0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef01234567"

requested_ref stores the exact tag or ref used by that repository. Some repos use tags like v1.2.0; others use 1.2.0.

For local plugin development, the active override lives in ~/.titan/config.toml:

[plugins.custom.source]
channel = "dev_local"
path = "/absolute/path/to/local/plugin/repo"

In dev_local, Titan loads the plugin directly from the local repository. In stable, Titan prepares an isolated local runtime for the pinned commit.
